chapters.title: Lead Like Lives Depend On It

articles.title: Nair joins Fenergo executive team to scale global revenue - FinTech Global
articles.snippet: Nair joins Fenergo executive team to scale global revenue  FinTech Global

articles.title: Ciena bolsters executive team with supply chain, product appointments to scale AI infrastructure - SDxCentral
articles.snippet: Ciena bolsters executive team with supply chain, product appointments to scale AI infrastructure  SDxCentral

articles.title: Buffini & Company adds three senior executives to scale coaching - HousingWire
articles.snippet: Buffini & Company adds three senior executives to scale coaching  HousingWire

post1.text:
Most companies don't stall at $50M because of strategy. They stall because leadership didn't scale with the business.

When Ciena recently restructured its executive team to handle AI infrastructure growth, the story wasn't about the hires themselves. It was about a leadership model that had outgrown its original design. That's the real signal.

Here's what leaders at $50M+ need to understand:

1. The skills that got you here are not the skills that get you to $500M. What made you decisive at $30M can make you a bottleneck at $100M.

2. Adding executives is not the same as building leadership capacity. Titles don't automatically create alignment or accountability.

3. Your leadership structure needs to be designed for where you're going, not where you've been. Most companies rebuild it too late.

The companies that scale successfully treat leadership architecture as a competitive advantage, not an org chart exercise.

What part of your leadership model were you never meant to outgrow but haven't updated yet?

post2.text:
A recent piece in SDxCentral caught our attention. Ciena just added senior executives in supply chain and product specifically to scale AI infrastructure. Smart move.

Here's the angle most companies miss: Ciena didn't wait until the cracks showed. They restructured leadership in anticipation of where the business was heading.

At Autobahn, we see the opposite pattern constantly. Companies between $50M and $200M hire reactively. A function breaks, then they backfill. By then, momentum is already lost.

If you're scaling aggressively, the question isn't who you need right now. It's who your company needs to become at the next stage and whether your current leadership team can get you there.

Read the full article here: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMixwFBVV95cUxOQm05NW1RSUh0Uk1fa0hYMU9QTHVnaXJhZEpfdVVjaE9uWURQS3QtNGxCMWZhWjNwdWUyeXFwengxbDZZRFd6OF91ZTdtUmtNSGoyTkRzdklfeENYQ09ITWN6UjhWQVE0UE5sQmx3MXZJdmNjLVhSM2plUXl5aXhyOS1aS2hCb2xYQVU4UUdDakphV3NPWTdfazVQVWJtUEs2bFJWY3VzeTk5N2tVN3dFdE5TOFlqMUZxaWZvdTRfQ2w2QmVOUFF3?oc=5

post3.text:
When Buffini and Company brought on three senior executives to scale their coaching business, they made a leadership bet. Not just a hiring decision.

That distinction matters more than most leaders realize.

In Chapter 1 of Rock Your Business, "Lead Like Lives Depend On It," Katherine Slain addresses exactly this tension. At a certain stage of growth, leadership stops being about personal output and starts being about the environment you create for others to perform.

The chapter doesn't just challenge you to lead better. It challenges you to examine whether your current identity as a leader is actually the ceiling on your company's growth.

That's an uncomfortable question. It's also the right one.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: There's a version of leadership that works perfectly at $30M and quietly destroys companies at $150M. The behaviors, instincts, and structures that built your early success have an expiration date. Most founders never see it coming because the warning signs look like growth. Here's what scaling leaders actually need to change before the business forces them to.
posts.email_subject: Your leadership has an expiration date

chapters.title: Maximize Your Multiple to Achieve Your Exit

articles.title: How to Create an Exit Strategy for Your Business - U.S. Chamber of Commerce
articles.snippet: How to Create an Exit Strategy for Your Business  U.S. Chamber of Commerce

articles.title: The Essential Elements Of Successful Exit Planning - Forbes
articles.snippet: The Essential Elements Of Successful Exit Planning  Forbes

post1.text:
Most business owners think about their exit six months before they want out. That is already too late.

Your multiple is not determined at the closing table. It is built over years through the decisions you make right now. Forbes recently broke down why the most successful exits share one thing in common: disciplined preparation long before any buyer shows up.

Here is what that actually looks like for a $50M+ company:

1. Clean up your financials before you have to. Buyers pay premiums for predictability, not potential. If your numbers require a 30-minute explanation, your multiple shrinks.

2. Reduce owner dependency. If the business cannot run without you for 90 days, you are not selling a company. You are selling a job.

3. Know your EBITDA levers. Not every dollar of revenue is equal in the eyes of a buyer. Recurring revenue, margin quality, and customer concentration all move the number.

The companies that maximize their exit are the ones that run like they are always for sale, even when they are not.

What would have to be true about your business today for a buyer to pay top dollar tomorrow?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Forbes on exit planning made something very clear: most business owners treat exit planning as a destination rather than a discipline.

For companies in the $50M to $500M range, that distinction is expensive.

At Autobahn, we see this constantly. Leaders who wait until they are ready to sell start discovering problems that should have been solved years earlier. Customer concentration too high. Management team too thin. Financials too dependent on one-time revenue.

The companies that achieve the strongest multiples are the ones that build exit readiness into how they operate every quarter, not just in the final sprint before going to market.

If you are scaling a business and have not mapped your value drivers lately, this article is worth your time.

https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMiogFBVV95cUxPQjFIYXhibGVtZGsyVmtwMGZpTjdobTVlcEJOMURid0JybUZzRjlzc212dU85c29LdEFYMXJDRU1vNzYtVXZIZzFhZVU0T3RpdzZ5VnR2bkZ2ZXJPRG83d1hiTFJ4UF85WHR5QnNJc0wzdEdqS0xsQXdFVi1JeG0zSGxYY3ZtX1ZUYkxWaDRpcE9sTENEVlNuN1pKTkxzZmRyOGc

post3.text:
Exit planning conversations have been everywhere lately, and for good reason. But most of what gets written skims the surface.

Chapter 2 of Rock Your Business goes deeper. Titled "Maximize Your Multiple to Achieve Your Exit," it was written by Paul Belair and George Hohman, two practitioners who have sat on both sides of the deal table with companies in the $50M to $500M range.

One thing they make clear: your multiple is not a result of how well you negotiate at closing. It is a result of how intentionally you have built the business over time. There is a specific set of value drivers that sophisticated buyers look for, and most sellers do not know what they are until it is too late to act on them.

This chapter gives you the framework to close that gap before you ever take a call from a buyer.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most business owners spend years building a company and weeks planning the exit. That imbalance costs them millions. The multiple you receive when you sell is not decided in a negotiation room. It is the result of every operational, financial, and leadership decision you made in the years before any buyer came looking. Here is what separates the companies that maximize their exit from the ones that leave money on the table.
posts.email_subject: Your exit multiple is already being decided

    "type": "thought_leadership",
    "text": "Most strategic plans fail not because the strategy was wrong, but because execution was treated as an afterthought.

Forbes recently explored why leadership teams consistently break down at the execution stage, and the findings point to a pattern scaling companies know well: the gap between deciding what to do and actually doing it widens as organizations grow.

Here is what leaders at $50M and beyond need to hear:

1. Your strategy is not the plan. The plan is how your team behaves on Tuesday morning when priorities compete.

2. Accountability without visibility is theater. If your team cannot see progress in real time, you are managing by hope.

3. Scaling amplifies misalignment. What worked as a $20M company will actively work against you at $100M if you have not rebuilt how decisions get made.

The companies that close this gap treat execution as a discipline, not a follow-up conversation after the offsite.

What is the single biggest point where your strategy breaks down between the boardroom and the front line?",
    "hashtags": ["#StrategicPlanning", "#ScalingBusiness", "#ExecutionExcellence"]
  },

At Autobahn, we see this constantly with companies scaling through the $50M to $200M range. Leaders build rigid 90-day plans and then punish their teams for adapting when conditions change. That is backwards.

The companies that scale well teach their people the intent behind the strategy, not just the steps. When the market shifts, those teams adapt without losing direction.

If your execution model requires perfect conditions to work, it is not a strategy. It is a wish list.

Read the full piece and think about how much room your current plan leaves for smart, real-time decisions: https://news.google.",

One of the sharpest insights in the chapter: the systems that got you to $50M are often the exact systems that will cap you there. Recognizing that inflection point, before it becomes a crisis, is what separates companies that scale

chapters.title: Every Day Without a Real Sales Strategy is a Donation

articles.title: SeedX Launches Specialized B2B Fintech Marketing Services to Drive Predictable Revenue Growth - The Manila Times
articles.snippet: SeedX Launches Specialized B2B Fintech Marketing Services to Drive Predictable Revenue Growth  The Manila Times

articles.title: AI Time Savings Won’t Drive Revenue Unless CSOs Intervene - Gartner
articles.snippet: AI Time Savings Won’t Drive Revenue Unless CSOs Intervene  Gartner

articles.title: B2B Sales Strategy Now Demands AI, Personalization to Survive - abasto.com
articles.snippet: B2B Sales Strategy Now Demands AI, Personalization to Survive  abasto.com

post1.text:
Most $50M+ companies don't have a sales strategy. They have a sales habit.

There's a difference. A habit is what your team does because it worked three years ago. A strategy is a deliberate system designed around where revenue actually comes from and why.

Gartner recently found that AI-driven time savings in sales are not translating into revenue growth unless chief sales officers actively reshape how that saved time gets used. That is not a technology problem. That is a strategy problem.

Here is what business leaders at this scale need to hear:

1. Activity without direction is expensive. More calls, more demos, more hustle does not compound. A clear strategy does.

2. Your sales motion needs to match your buyer's actual decision process, not the one you assume they have.

3. Every quarter you operate without a defined sales strategy is a quarter you are funding your competitor's growth.

What would change in your business if you treated your sales strategy with the same rigor as your financial plan?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Gartner makes a point that every scaling company should sit with: AI is saving sales teams significant time, but that time is not automatically converting into revenue. The reason is a lack of strategic direction from the top.

This is exactly what we see with companies in the $50M to $500M range. They adopt tools, they optimize workflows, and they still miss their numbers. Why? Because tools execute strategy. They do not replace it.

If your chief sales officer or sales leadership team cannot clearly articulate where your revenue is coming from and why, no amount of AI efficiency will close that gap.

Read the Gartner piece and ask yourself honestly: do we have a real sales strategy or just a faster version of an old habit?

https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMiYEFVX3lxTE83YmJZYVNxUUtqV1lJanRha3hGWnFtdjJYSm5FWURzTUFrVm1uTDB2R1VRWklWVlM4cVhURDNTMVFxVmw0Ql9YZUZrdndhcC1JWFRZczVJRmxlR1REQmVvMg

post3.text:
A recent report from Gartner confirmed what we have been saying for years: time saved in sales does not automatically become revenue. Leadership has to connect the dots.

Chapter 4 of our upcoming book, Rock Your Business, is titled "Every Day Without a Real Sales Strategy is a Donation." Authors Deb and Brandon lay out something that stops most leadership teams cold: the cost of a vague sales strategy is not just slower growth, it is a direct, ongoing transfer of opportunity to your competition.

The chapter introduces a framework that forces leaders to pressure-test whether what they call a sales strategy is actually one. Most discover they have been running on assumptions, not architecture.

If your sales results feel inconsistent or hard to predict, this chapter was written for you.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Your company does not have a sales strategy problem. It has a strategy-shaped habit wearing the word strategy like a costume. For companies scaling past $50 million, that distinction is worth millions of dollars annually. The research is catching up to what high-growth operators already know: without deliberate sales architecture at the leadership level, every efficiency gain is just noise.
posts.email_subject: Your sales strategy is a donation

  "post1": {
    "type": "thought_leadership",
    "text": "Spending more on media does not make your brand stronger. It just makes it louder.

Unilever recently confirmed what sharp operators already suspect: cultural relevance built through creators is outperforming raw media spend when it comes to actual brand growth. That should stop every $50M+ executive in their tracks.

Here is what that means in practice:

First, attention is not the same as trust. You can buy impressions all day. You cannot buy the credibility that comes from someone your audience already follows saying your brand belongs in their world.

Second, your brand identity has to be strong enough that others can carry it authentically. If your own team cannot articulate what your company stands for, no creator can save you.

Third, scaling companies often mistake marketing investment for marketing strategy. More budget amplifies whatever signal already exists. If that signal is weak or unclear, you are just accelerating the wrong message.

Brand is not a department. It is the reputation your company builds whether you are paying attention or not.

What does your brand smell like to someone who has never heard of you?",
    "hashtags": ["#BrandStrategy", "#ScalingBusiness", "#MarketingLeadership"]
  },
  "post2": {
    "type": "article_reaction",
    "text": "A recent piece in Storyboard18 caught our attention. Unilever is publicly crediting creator-driven cultural relevance, not media spend, as the primary engine behind brand growth right now.

For companies scaling from $50M to $500M, this is not a trend to file away. It is a strategic signal.

At that growth stage, most leadership teams are still treating marketing like a volume game. Bigger budget, bigger reach, bigger results. But Unilever's position tells a different story: relevance compounds, spend does not.

The real question for scaling companies is whether your brand has a clear enough identity that outside voices can represent it credibly. If your positioning is fuzzy internally, it will be invisible externally.

Read the full piece and think about where your marketing dollars are actually building equity versus just buying noise.

{
  "post1": {
    "type": "thought_leadership",
    "text": "Hiring faster is not your talent problem. Hiring the wrong people faster is.

Most companies between $50M and $200M treat talent acquisition like a logistics function. Post the role, screen resumes, fill the seat. Then they wonder why growth stalls at the exact moment it should be accelerating.

Recent moves by major organizations to appoint dedicated, senior-level talent acquisition leaders signal something worth paying attention to: the companies serious about scale are treating hiring as a strategic function, not an administrative one.

Here is what that means in practice for leaders at your stage:

1. Your talent strategy should be written before your org chart is. If hiring reacts to structure, you will always be behind.

2. Retention is a sourcing strategy. Every strong performer you keep is a role you do not have to fill during a growth sprint.

3. The person owning talent acquisition should be in the room when business strategy is set, not briefed after.

At $50M, you can survive a bad hire. At $200M, it can cost you a market.

Who in your company owns talent strategy, and do they have the authority to match that responsibility?",
    "hashtags": ["#TalentStrategy", "#ScalingBusiness", "#PeopleFirst"]
  },
  "post2": {
    "type": "article_reaction",
    "text": "A recent piece in Business Wire caught our attention: Jobsity acquired Eureka Labs specifically to expand its nearshore talent capacity and deepen its value as a talent partner at scale.

This is not just an M&A story. It is a signal.

Scaling companies are no longer waiting to find talent. They are acquiring the infrastructure to access it faster.

For leaders growing through the $50M to $500M range, the lesson is direct: talent constraints will slow growth before capital constraints do. The organizations pulling ahead are treating talent access as a competitive asset, not a staffing function.

If your current approach to building your team is reactive, this is a good moment to ask whether it can support where you are going, not just where you are.

Read the full story here: https://news.google.",
    "source_url": "https://news.google."
  },
  "post3": {
    "type": "book_tie_in",
    "text": "When Relativity Space brought in a new Director of Talent Acquisition, it was not just a staffing decision. It was a growth decision.

That distinction is exactly what Chapter 6 of Rock Your Business tackles head-on.

The chapter is called The Talent Engine, and co-author Caleb Hawkins makes one thing clear: at the $50M to $500M growth stage, talent is not a support function. It is the engine itself. Get it right and everything accelerates. Let it drift and no amount of strategy or capital covers the gap.

Chapter 6 walks leaders through how to build a talent system that scales with the business, not one that constantly plays catch-up to it. There is a specific framework inside that will change how you think about the roles you hire for first.

We will leave the details for the book.

Rock Your Business, by Jonathan Slain and Katherine Sl

chapters.title: Death by KPIs
chapters.authors: Héctor Arias, Matt Radicelli & Jim Randall

articles.title: 14 Tools to Track KPIs for Your Business - Business.com
articles.snippet: 14 Tools to Track KPIs for Your Business  Business.com

articles.title: 30 Financial Metrics and KPIs to Measure Success in 2026 - Oracle NetSuite
articles.snippet: 30 Financial Metrics and KPIs to Measure Success in 2026  Oracle NetSuite

articles.title: The KPIs to Help Manufacturers Improve Company Performance - CliftonLarsonAllen (CLA)
articles.snippet: The KPIs to Help Manufacturers Improve Company Performance  CliftonLarsonAllen (CLA)

post1.text:
More KPIs do not make better decisions. They make slower ones.

Most companies scaling past $50M fall into the same trap: they add metrics every quarter and never subtract any. The dashboard grows. The clarity shrinks. Leaders spend more time explaining numbers than acting on them.

A recent Business.com breakdown of KPI tracking tools reinforced something we see constantly with clients: the problem is rarely a lack of data. It is a lack of discipline about which data actually drives decisions at your stage of growth.

Three things worth considering if your company is in the $50M to $500M range:

First, if a metric does not change a decision, it is decoration. Cut it.

Second, the right number of KPIs for a leadership team is usually far smaller than what is currently on your reporting stack.

Third, when everyone owns a KPI, no one owns accountability. Assign one name to each number.

How many of your current KPIs would survive a honest audit?

post2.text:
A recent piece from Oracle NetSuite outlines 30 financial metrics and KPIs companies should consider tracking heading into 2026. It is a solid reference list, and worth a read.

But here is the honest challenge for companies in the $50M to $500M range: a list of 30 metrics is not a strategy. It is a starting point that can quietly become a liability.

At Autobahn, we work with leaders who are drowning in reporting they built when their company was half the size. The metrics made sense then. They are now noise.

Scaling requires you to be ruthless about what you measure and why. The companies that grow fastest are not tracking more. They are tracking better.

Read the full article and then ask: which of these 30 actually live on your leadership scorecard?

https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMikwFBVV95cUxNcThuZllxNkVka0dpOV9QdHZQM2RiU0tfMGVIR05tdW9ZN1AtYngwQ1d4WDFSb3ZNSUxKNHFCSUR4aC1lR1g2ZWYzWFE0NWF1WmVpejAxOW9VRFdJa01yLUFKa1hvbjNGQnR6a2hZcVVtQzgwRXNCdXVGbjhfNWR2eWpTSlFyRk5zLVZ3MnJvMU14ZjA

post3.text:
CliftonLarsonAllen recently published a guide on the KPIs manufacturers should use to improve performance. Good tactical advice. But it points to a bigger question that Chapter 7 of our upcoming book addresses head-on.

Chapter 7 is called Death by KPIs. It was written by Héctor Arias, Matt Radicelli, and Jim Randall, and it tackles something most leaders will not say out loud: the metrics system you built to run your business might now be the thing holding it back.

One insight from the chapter worth sitting with: there is a meaningful difference between measuring activity and measuring momentum. Most companies have built scorecards that do the former while thinking they are doing the latter.

If your team dreads the weekly metrics review, that is a signal worth paying attention to.

Rock Your Business, our second book from Jonathan Slain and Katherine Slain, is available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most leadership teams believe they have a data problem. They do not. They have a discipline problem. When companies scale past $50M, KPI dashboards tend to grow faster than the clarity they were supposed to create. Chapter 7 of Rock Your Business calls this Death by KPIs, and it is more common than any executive wants to admit. Here is what to do about it.
posts.email_subject: Are your KPIs killing your growth?

articles.title: AI productivity gains are real but so is bad management: 'Leaders are really struggling to articulate what the vision and strategy is' - Fortune
articles.snippet: AI productivity gains are real but so is bad management: 'Leaders are really struggling to articulate what the vision and strategy is'  Fortune

post1.text:
AI will not save your business. Your strategy will.

Every week, another headline promises that AI tools will transform how executives work. And the productivity gains are real. Bloomberg recently surveyed twelve CEOs and found genuine time savings across routine tasks, communication, and research. That part is true.

But here is what the headlines skip: AI amplifies whatever leadership capacity already exists in your organization. If your team lacks clarity on direction, AI gives them the ability to move faster in the wrong direction.

For leaders scaling past $50M, three things actually matter here:

1. AI does not replace the need for a clear vision. It exposes the absence of one faster than ever before.

2. The biggest productivity gains come from how you structure your team around AI tools, not from the tools themselves.

3. Leaders who treat AI as a strategy are outsourcing their thinking. Leaders who treat it as infrastructure are building leverage.

So before you add another AI subscription to your tech stack, ask yourself this: does your team know exactly where you are going without you in the room?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Fortune put it plainly: AI productivity gains are real, but bad management cancels them out. Leaders are struggling to articulate vision and strategy, and AI is making that gap impossible to hide.

At Autobahn, we see this pattern constantly with companies in the $50M to $500M range. The technology is rarely the problem. The operating clarity is.

When your leadership team cannot align around a coherent direction, AI tools become expensive noise generators. When they can, those same tools become serious competitive leverage.

If your company is scaling and you are adding AI capabilities before locking in your strategic foundation, you may be building fast on unstable ground.

Read the Fortune piece and then ask your leadership team how they would answer the vision question. That conversation will tell you more than any tool audit.

https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMitwFBVV95cUxQX3dVNkVCYVZTSTZBRlFWVVQ5cHFwSktudmFWRzZVNFhEb0R1eV91aDJVMWFUbklLWDBpSDM0SU8xQmUxa0w5czNuWWUycG1RckdxNzJySVZyREhOdE1uWjQ2bk14dG1zd0w3Tk1GcE5OY29ZcjQyS01ZdWd5LWpManR2enVOR2Zua1dleE1FNkJTVlpObGU4Z28zX1FIOEsxZWExMldmUFZfeEJIcHd2NWpDc3I3eFE

post3.text:
The New York Times recently covered a growing trend of executives using AI versions of themselves to handle meetings, emails, and decisions when they are unavailable. It sounds futuristic. It also raises a serious question about where human judgment ends and automation begins.

Chapter 8 of Rock Your Business, called The Third Monitor Theory, takes this head on. Co-authored by Hudson Shank and Jonathan Slain, the chapter lays out a framework for how scaling companies should think about integrating AI into daily operations without losing the leadership presence that drives culture and performance.

One thing the chapter makes clear: the executives winning with AI are not the ones using the most tools. They are the ones who have decided precisely where AI belongs and where it does not.

That line alone is worth the read.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: AI is not a productivity tool. It is a magnifier. For companies scaling past $50M, that distinction changes everything about how you deploy it. The leaders pulling ahead right now are not using more AI than their competitors. They are using it inside a strategy that was already clear. Here is what that actually looks like in practice.
posts.email_subject: AI won't save a fuzzy strategy

chapters.title: How Could You Be Successful and Not Have It?
chapters.authors: Samantha Frost, Caleb Hawkins & Rafa Loyo

articles.title: Six Ways to Overcome Impostor Syndrome in a New Leadership Role - Seattle University
articles.snippet: Six Ways to Overcome Impostor Syndrome in a New Leadership Role  Seattle University

articles.title: Don’t call it imposter syndrome - Fast Company
articles.snippet: Don’t call it imposter syndrome  Fast Company

articles.title: Impostor syndrome: the cost of being ‘superwoman’ at work and beyond - The Conversation
articles.snippet: Impostor syndrome: the cost of being ‘superwoman’ at work and beyond  The Conversation

post1.text:
The most dangerous leaders at the $50M+ level are not the ones who lack confidence. They are the ones who have learned to perform confidence so well that they stopped questioning themselves entirely.

Imposter syndrome gets treated like a problem to eliminate. It is not. At scale, a degree of self-doubt is a signal that you understand the complexity of what you are managing. Leaders who feel none of it tend to under-invest in the people and systems around them.

Research from Seattle University reinforces this: navigating imposter syndrome in leadership is less about silencing doubt and more about building the right structures and relationships that make doubt productive rather than paralyzing.

Three things high-growth leaders should consider:

First, separate performance anxiety from genuine skill gaps. They require different responses.

Second, your leadership team should include people who will challenge your assumptions, not validate them.

Third, the higher your revenue, the more your internal narrative shapes company culture whether you intend it to or not.

What would change in your organization if your leadership team treated uncertainty as information instead of weakness?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Fast Company makes a pointed argument: the term imposter syndrome may be doing more harm than good. The label, the author suggests, puts the burden of fix-it thinking on the individual when the environment often deserves equal scrutiny.

For companies scaling past $50M, this distinction matters enormously. Leaders at that stage are not just managing their own psychology. They are building cultures. If your organization's response to self-doubt is to tell people to push through it privately, you are leaving real performance and retention problems unaddressed.

At Autobahn, we see this pattern repeatedly in high-growth companies. The internal narrative of leadership becomes the unwritten operating system of the whole organization.

Worth reading if you lead a team through rapid change.

https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMidkFVX3lxTE9PRnBrU1NWWm1BeEl3dk5qVjFRc0M0T2ctZW9fbjdWd2NmdFlUZVVNeW9JV0VTWkktVmp0MlVjbFVEMXEweWZBS1d6bFBSMVNoNFVfWm5WaFo0aG1PT1RJN0p2Ylc0SkRONnBBcDYzZmNEUUJsRFE

post3.text:
Chapter 9 of Rock Your Business is titled "How Could You Be Successful and Not Have It?" and it tackles something most business books avoid entirely: the internal experience of leading a company through serious growth.

Authored by Samantha Frost, Caleb Hawkins, and Rafa Loyo, the chapter confronts a pattern seen consistently in leaders scaling past $50M. Success does not dissolve self-doubt. For many leaders, it intensifies it. The stakes are higher. The visibility is greater. The gap between how others see you and how you see yourself can quietly undermine your decision-making in ways that are hard to detect from the outside.

The chapter does not offer a quick fix. It offers a framework for understanding why this happens at this stage of growth specifically, and what high-performing leaders do differently when they encounter it.

Rock Your Business — available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Every leader who has scaled a company past $50M has felt it: the quiet, persistent sense that the results do not fully belong to them. Most never say it out loud. That silence has a cost. The psychology of leadership at scale is not a soft topic. It is an operational one, and the companies that treat it that way outperform the ones that do not.
posts.email_subject: The self-doubt your P&L doesn't show

articles.title: Karen Powell, Founder of My Wealth 4 Life, Interviewed on the Influential Entrepreneurs Podcast, Discussing Securing Retirement Lifestyle - redlandscommunitynews.com
articles.snippet: Karen Powell, Founder of My Wealth 4 Life, Interviewed on the Influential Entrepreneurs Podcast, Discussing Securing Retirement Lifestyle  redlandscommunitynews.com

post1.text:
Most founders treat life planning like a retirement project. That is a mistake that costs them everything.

When Morgan Stanley surveyed entrepreneurs after major liquidity events, the pattern was consistent: the leaders who struggled most had optimized entirely for the exit and nothing beyond it. The business became the life. Then the business was gone.

For $50M+ leaders still in the building phase, three things matter now, not later:

1. Define what a full life looks like before the pressure of a transaction forces you to decide under duress.

2. Separate your identity from your revenue number. The company's growth ceiling is often the founder's personal ceiling in disguise.

3. Build recovery into your operating calendar the same way you build in board meetings. Non-negotiable, scheduled, protected.

Scaling a business is not the opposite of living well. But it will crowd out everything else if you let it run without boundaries.

What would you stop tolerating in your schedule if you actually treated your life as a strategic priority?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Fortune explored what life looks like for entrepreneurs after they sell their business, and the findings from Morgan Stanley are worth paying attention to before the exit, not after.

The core tension: founders spend years building toward a number, then discover they never built toward a life.

At Autobahn, we see this dynamic show up well before any transaction. Leaders scaling from $50M to $500M often defer every personal priority until some future milestone arrives. That deferral becomes a habit. And habits do not automatically reverse when a deal closes.

The companies that scale cleanly tend to be led by people who have done this inner work while still in the game.

If you are in a growth phase right now, this article is worth 10 minutes of honest reflection.

https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMiiwFBVV95cUxPdnZpWG94aDF5el9jOUtxNWhVYlFXZ3FKejZHQkJEOTVVSmN2VE1COEtTb3lkTnJ1VENGTVoxWGUzOHhYdlJ4QUV6bW9IajBVTEQ2cm5vUWRST3pCcWlxXzNzNHNMQlRmSURudzZZbFNuWkx2YUZWakN5ME1mcVU0Rm9XcFdMOXQzNFo4?oc=5

post3.text:
Scaling a company from $50M to $500M is a full-contact sport. But Chapter 10 of our upcoming book asks a question most growth-stage leaders avoid: what are you actually building this for?

The chapter is called Coming Up for Air. It was written by Samantha Frost alongside Jonathan and Katherine Slain, and it tackles life planning not as a soft topic but as a structural one.

One insight that lands hard for most readers: the way you design your time right now, during the scale, is practice for the life you will live after it. Most leaders are practicing the wrong things without realizing it.

There is a framework inside this chapter that helps leaders get intentional before the business makes all the decisions for them.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: You will spend years engineering your company's growth. You will build financial models, hire leadership teams, and map out five-year trajectories. But most founders never spend a single hour designing the life those years are supposed to produce. That gap is not a personal failure. It is a structural one, and it has a fix.
posts.email_subject: The plan you forgot to build
